title: Gut microbiota critically regulates neuronal function via gut-brain mechanisms,
content: Gut microbiota critically regulates neuronal function via gut-brain mechanisms, influencing neurogenesis, synaptic plasticity, and behavior. Precision nutrition modulating the gut-brain axis is promising for cognitive health and neurodegeneration prevention.

source.title: Precision Nutrition and Gut–Brain Axis Modulation in Prevention of Neurodegenerative Diseases (Nutrients 2025)

title: GI tract communicates bidirectionally with CNS through neural (vagus nerve), end
content: GI tract communicates bidirectionally with CNS through neural (vagus nerve), endocrine (HPA axis), immune (cytokines), and metabolic pathways. Nutrition modulates neurological health via this gut-brain axis in both physiological and pathological states.

content: Nutrients 2025, 17, 3068 24 of 39 Recent studies have demonstrated that circadian regulation disorders, including desyn- chronosis, can initiate oxidative stress [231], neuroinflammation [232], and mitochondrial dysfunction [233]—key pathogenetic mechanisms of neurodegenerative diseases. title: Understanding Nutrition 26 Risk factors for chronic diseases Factors that increa
content: Understanding Nutrition 26 Risk factors for chronic diseases Factors that increase or reduce the risk of developing chronic diseases can be identified by analysing statistical data. A strong association between a risk factor and a disease means that when the factor is present, the likelihood of developing the disease increases. It does not mean that all people with the risk factor will develop the disease. Similarly, a lack of risk factors does not guarantee freedom from a given disease. O

title: Understanding Nutrition 40 Chapter 1 explained that the body’s many activities a
content: Understanding Nutrition 40 Chapter 1 explained that the body’s many activities are supported by the nutrients delivered by the foods we eat. Food choices made over years influence the body’s health, with consistently poor choices increasing the risks of developing chronic disease in later life. This chapter shows how we can choose from the tens of thousands of available foods to create a diet that supports health. Most foods provide several nutrients, so wise diet planning involves selecting

title: Understanding Nutrition 42 Foods that are notably low in nutrient density, such
content: Understanding Nutrition 42 Foods that are notably low in nutrient density, such as potato chips, lollies and soft drinks, are sometimes called empty-kilojoule foods. The kilojoules these foods provide are called ‘empty’ because they deliver energy (from sugar, fat or both) with little or no protein, vitamins or minerals. The concept of nutrient density is relatively simple when examining the contributions of one nutrient to a food or diet. With respect to calcium, milk ranks high and meats

content: Understanding Nutrition 44 2.2 Diet-planning guides To plan a diet that achieves all the dietary ideals just outlined, we need tools as well as knowledge. Among the most widely used tools for diet planning are food group plans that build a diet from groups of foods that are similar in nutrient content. content: Understanding Nutrition 48 All vegetables provide an array of vitamins, fibre and the mineral potassium, but some vegetables are especially good sources of certain nutrients, and of beneficial phytochemicals – the non-nutrient compounds found in plant-derived foods that have biological activity in the body. content: Chapter 2: Planning a healthy diet 49 Dietary guidelines prescribe a set diet. FALSE juice. Oil is a notable exception: even though oil is pure fat and therefore rich in kilojoules, a small amount of oil from sources such as nuts, fish or vegetable oils is necessary every day to provide nutrients lacking from other foods. Consequently, these high-fat foods are listed among the nutrient-dense foods (see Highlight 5 to learn why). Serving equivalents Recommended serving amounts for fruits, ve

title: Understanding Nutrition 50 We can begin to fill in the plan with real foods to c
content: Understanding Nutrition 50 We can begin to fill in the plan with real foods to create an eating pattern to sustain good health. For example, breakfast calls for 30 grams of grain, ½ cup fruit and 1 cup of milk. We might select a bowl of cereal with banana slices and milk: • 1 cup cereal = 30 grams grain • 1 small banana = ½ cup fruit • 1 cup fat-free milk = 1 cup milk. We can then continue to create a diet plan by establishing healthy eating patterns for lunch, dinner and other snacks.

title: Seven out of every ten deaths in the US are attributed to chronic diseases such
content: Seven out of every ten deaths in the US are attributed to chronic diseases such as cancer, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, all of which have a direct nutritional connection. A randomized controlled trial published in JAMA (2003) found that adding soy protein, viscous fiber, and nuts can be as effective for lowering cholesterol as adding a prescription statin medication to a low-saturated fat diet. Furthermore, a New England Journal of Medicine study (2002) showed that diet and lifestyle changes decreased diabetes risk by 58%, compared to only 31% for metformin. Participants following lifestyle changes also had a significant reduction in C-reactive protein (CRP), an indirect marker of subclinical inflammation.

source.title: The Anti-Inflammation Diet and Recipe Book

title: According to Henry Lindlahr, M.D., diseases are often the result of nutrient def
content: According to Henry Lindlahr, M.D., diseases are often the result of nutrient deficiency, resulting in delivery by blood and lymph of less than adequate nutrients to fuel cell metabolism. If the diet consists of an excess of low-quality proteins, carbohydrates, and fats, tissues will inevitably be clogged with 'morbid matter.' This toxin accumulation can interfere with vital cellular functions, becoming pathogenic. The buildup of toxins supports cellular degeneration, moving the system toward disease rather than supporting cellular regeneration needed for healing.

source.title: The Anti-Inflammation Diet and Recipe Book

title: The terrain theory states that disease is only able to affect a body that is sus
content: The terrain theory states that disease is only able to affect a body that is susceptible. Dr. Rene Dubos, renowned microbiologist at the Rockefeller Institute, stated: 'Viruses and bacteria are not the cause of the disease; there is something else. Most human beings carry throughout life a variety of microbial agents potentially pathogenic for them; only when something happens which upsets the equilibrium between host and parasite does infection develop into disease.' The body's terrain determines susceptibility based on diet, lifestyle, genetic history, emotional stress, environmental toxin overload, and physical activity.

source.title: The Anti-Inflammation Diet and Recipe Book

title: The Elimination and Challenge Diet protocol: Strictly follow the anti-inflammati
content: The Elimination and Challenge Diet protocol: Strictly follow the anti-inflammation diet for four weeks. After that time, each food can be introduced individually in its most whole form. For example, to introduce tomatoes, eat one whole tomato after avoiding anything with tomatoes for four weeks; do not use tomato sauce or soup. After eating the tomato, don't eat any more tomatoes or tomato products for three days because some reactions may be delayed. After three days with no reaction, tomatoes are probably safe. Then introduce the next food in whole form, wait three days, and so on. If you have a reaction immediately, avoid that food and introduce the next food without waiting the usual 3 days. Reactions can be anything from mild congestion to mood disturbance to severe abdominal cramps.

source.title: The Anti-Inflammation Diet and Recipe Book
source.chapter: Chapter 3: The Importance of Diet - Elimination Diet Protocol

title: Recommended macronutrient proportions for optimal health: Protein 20-30% of tota
content: Recommended macronutrient proportions for optimal health: Protein 20-30% of total calories, Fat (primarily a balance of essential fatty acids) 20-30% of total calories, Carbohydrate (primarily complex carbohydrates) 40-60% of total calories. Adult protein intake should equal 45-60 grams per day. The low-fat, low-calorie diet often proposed for weight loss typically ends in a perpetual cycle of weight loss and regain. You should not have to greatly restrict calorie intake to lose weight if you are eating the right foods. Eating enough good-quality protein at each meal is one of the best ways to maintain steady blood sugar, energy levels, and mood throughout the day.

source.title: The Anti-Inflammation Diet and Recipe Book

title: Lifestyle and mealtime recommendations: Do not eat any one food more than five t
content: Lifestyle and mealtime recommendations: Do not eat any one food more than five times per week. Eat at regularly scheduled times to establish patterns with the endocrine system facilitating digestion. Do not eat late at night when metabolism is getting ready to rest. Eat a whole-grain breakfast (related to 15% reduction in insulin resistance risk). Do not drink anything while eating; if necessary, only small sips of water. Have a quiet, relaxed atmosphere - do not watch TV, drive, or talk on the phone while eating. Chew food at least 19 times before swallowing. Spend time with your food and reflect on the energy it holds. Fast food accounts for 40% of Americans' dietary intake. A study from Brigham and Women's Hospital (2006) found that preschoolers who regularly ate French fries had increased breast cancer risk - each additional serving per week as a preschooler increased risk by 27%.

source.title: The Anti-Inflammation Diet and Recipe Book

title: Three fundamental principles of healthy eating: Variety (eating many different f
content: Three fundamental principles of healthy eating: Variety (eating many different foods from all food groups to ensure adequate intake of all nutrients and phytochemicals, and to prevent excessive exposure to any single contaminant or anti-nutrient), Moderation (consuming appropriate portion sizes and limiting intake of foods high in added sugars, saturated fats, and sodium), and Balance (ensuring caloric intake matches energy expenditure and that nutrient proportions align with recommendations). The 2020-2025 Dietary Guidelines for Americans emphasize a life-stage approach, recognizing that nutritional needs change throughout the lifespan from infancy through older adulthood. MyPlate translates these guidelines into practical visual guidance: fill half the plate with fruits and vegetables, a quarter with whole grains, and a quarter with lean protein, with dairy on the side.

source.title: NUTRITION (BYU-Idaho)
